---
title: "How to Pull the Right Lever"
subtitle: "School Attainment, Open Data Analytics and Policy in England — Lessons from Brighton and Hove"
author:
  - name: Adam Dennett
    affiliations: "Bartlett Centre for Advanced Spatial Analysis, UCL"
  - name: Dan Campbell-Meiklejohn
    affiliations: "School of Psychology, University of Sussex"
date: today
title-slide-attributes:
  data-background-image: "slides_assets/title-bg.png"
  data-background-size: "cover"
  data-background-position: "center"
  data-background-opacity: "1"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, ucl-slides-theme.scss]
    logo: slides_assets/ucl-logo-purple.png
    slide-number: true
    progress: true
    hash: true
    navigation-mode: linear
    controls: true
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    margin: 0.08
    fig-cap-location: bottom
    fig-width: 10
    fig-height: 5.5
    fig-dpi: 150
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Code"
    embed-resources: true
    auto-stretch: true
    include-after-body: slides-footer.html
    output-file: "pulling_the_right_lever_slides.html"
output-dir: docs
bibliography: SchoolAttainment.bib
csl: sage-harvard.csl
suppress-bibliography: true
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

```{r setup}
#| label: setup
#| include: false

library(tidyverse)
library(lme4)
library(lmerTest)
library(performance)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
library(scales)
library(here)
library(ggrepel)
library(sf)
library(leaflet)

source(here::here("R", "helpers.R"))

# ---- Load data ----
panel <- readRDS(here::here("data", "panel_data.rds"))
HIGHLIGHT_LA <- "Brighton and Hove"

models_imputed <- readRDS(here::here("data", "models_imputed.rds"))
mod_e_all       <- models_imputed$all
mod_e_disadv    <- models_imputed$disadvantaged
mod_e_nondisadv <- models_imputed$non_disadvantaged

# ---- Prepare model dataset ----
imputed_full_data <- panel %>%
  filter(MINORGROUP %in% c("Academy", "Maintained school")) %>%
  filter(
    !is.na(ATT8SCR), ATT8SCR > 0,
    PTFSM6CLA1A > 0, PERCTOT > 0, PNUMEAL > 0,
    !is.na(OFSTEDRATING_1), !is.na(gor_name), !is.na(LANAME)
  ) %>%
  filter(
    !is.na(remained_in_the_same_school),
    !is.na(teachers_on_leadership_pay_range_percent),
    average_number_of_days_taken > 0,
    !is.na(gorard_segregation)
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    OFSTEDRATING_1 = factor(OFSTEDRATING_1,
      levels = c("Outstanding", "Good", "Requires Improvement", "Inadequate"),
      ordered = TRUE),
    gor_name   = factor(gor_name),
    LANAME     = factor(LANAME),
    year_label = factor(year_label)
  ) %>%
  droplevels()

contrasts(imputed_full_data$OFSTEDRATING_1) <-
  contr.treatment(levels(imputed_full_data$OFSTEDRATING_1))

# ---- Brighton & Hove data ----
bh_data <- panel %>%
  filter(LANAME == HIGHLIGHT_LA,
         MINORGROUP %in% c("Academy", "Maintained school"))

bh_data <- bh_data %>%
  mutate(
    predicted_all = predict(mod_e_all, newdata = bh_data, allow.new.levels = TRUE),
    predicted_dis = predict(mod_e_disadv, newdata = bh_data, allow.new.levels = TRUE),
    predicted_non = predict(mod_e_nondisadv, newdata = bh_data, allow.new.levels = TRUE)
  )

bh_latest_model <- bh_data %>%
  filter(!is.na(ATT8SCR)) %>%
  group_by(SCHNAME) %>%
  filter(year_label == max(year_label)) %>%
  ungroup()

bh_latest <- bh_data %>%
  filter(!is.na(ATT8SCR)) %>%
  group_by(URN) %>%
  filter(year_label == max(year_label)) %>%
  ungroup()

# ---- National benchmarks ----
national_benchmarks <- panel %>%
  filter(MINORGROUP %in% c("Academy", "Maintained school"),
         !is.na(ATT8SCR), ATT8SCR > 0,
         PTFSM6CLA1A > 0, PERCTOT > 0, PNUMEAL > 0,
         !is.na(OFSTEDRATING_1))

latest_year <- max(bh_latest_model$year_label, na.rm = TRUE)
national_latest <- national_benchmarks %>% filter(year_label == latest_year)

national_mean_abs <- mean(national_latest$PERCTOT, na.rm = TRUE)
bh_mean_abs      <- mean(bh_latest_model$PERCTOT, na.rm = TRUE)
national_mean_fsm <- mean(national_latest$PTFSM6CLA1A, na.rm = TRUE)
bh_mean_fsm      <- mean(bh_latest_model$PTFSM6CLA1A, na.rm = TRUE)

# ---- Slide theme ----
theme_slide <- theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 16, colour = "#3A1857"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(colour = "grey40", size = 12),
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "bottom",
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = "#FAFAFA", colour = NA)
  )

# ---- Helper functions ----
relabel_terms <- function(term) {
  case_when(
    term == "(Intercept)" ~ "(Intercept)",
    term == "log(PTFSM6CLA1A)" ~ "log(% FSM)",
    term == "log(PERCTOT)" ~ "log(% Absence)",
    term == "log(PNUMEAL)" ~ "log(% EAL)",
    term == "PTPRIORLO" ~ "% Low Prior Attainment",
    term == "gorard_segregation" ~ "Gorard Segregation",
    term == "ADMPOL_PTOTHER NON SEL" ~ "Admissions: Other non-selective",
    term == "ADMPOL_PTSEL" ~ "Admissions: Selective",
    term == "remained_in_the_same_school" ~ "Teacher Retention",
    term == "teachers_on_leadership_pay_range_percent" ~ "Leadership Pay %",
    term == "log(average_number_of_days_taken)" ~ "log(Teacher Sickness)",
    TRUE ~ term
  )
}

# ---- LA rankings ----
la_means_abs <- national_benchmarks %>%
  filter(year_label == latest_year) %>%
  group_by(LANAME) %>%
  summarise(la_mean_abs = mean(PERCTOT, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  arrange(la_mean_abs) %>%
  mutate(rank = row_number(), pctile = rank / n() * 100)

la_means_fsm <- national_benchmarks %>%
  filter(year_label == latest_year) %>%
  group_by(LANAME) %>%
  summarise(la_mean_fsm = mean(PTFSM6CLA1A, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  arrange(la_mean_fsm) %>%
  mutate(rank = row_number(), pctile = rank / n() * 100)

n_las_bench <- nrow(la_means_abs)
bh_abs_rank <- la_means_abs %>% filter(grepl("Brighton", LANAME))
bh_fsm_rank <- la_means_fsm %>% filter(grepl("Brighton", LANAME))

# Model R2
r2_all <- performance::r2(mod_e_all)
r2_dis <- performance::r2(mod_e_disadv)
r2_non <- performance::r2(mod_e_nondisadv)
```

# Motivation & Context {background-color="#3A1857" .divider-slide}

## Why this paper?

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="55%"}

- The UK Government's **2026 Schools White Paper** sets ambitious targets:
  - Halve the disadvantage attainment gap
  - Improve attendance
  - Reform admissions
  - Tackle place-based disadvantage

- Achieving these requires LEAs to understand **local drivers** of attainment

- This paper is motivated by **direct experience** of policy development in Brighton and Hove in 2024--25

:::

::: {.column width="45%"}

::: {.key-finding}
**The problem:** A crucial gap exists between the *availability* of open data and the *availability* of accessible, contextualised intelligence for decision makers.
:::

::: {.key-finding}
**The risk:** Policy designed around an incomplete understanding of local attainment drivers can pull the wrong lever — doing little good and potentially causing harm.
:::

:::

::::

## Who we are

- **All residents** of Brighton and Hove with lived experience of the policy process

- Academics familiar with data and quantitative methods

- People experienced in national policy making

- School governors and teaching staff

- Parents and residents who want good education policy for **all** children

::: {.key-finding}
We bring diverse perspectives to an issue that affects thousands of families — and we believe better evidence tools can improve policy outcomes everywhere.
:::


## What are we trying to do?

::: {.incremental}

1. Show that **DfE open data** can explain ~80% of variation in school-level attainment with just a handful of variables

2. Identify the **relative importance** of different policy levers — and reveal which ones actually have mechanical advantage

3. Apply this to **Brighton and Hove** as a case study where policy pulled the wrong lever

4. Present a **Policy Simulator tool** that bridges the gap between data and decision making

:::

::: {.fragment}
::: {.key-finding}
We are **not** claiming the best possible model — we are showing that *good enough* models built from open data can transform the quality of local policy conversations.
:::
:::


# The Literature: What Drives Attainment? {background-color="#3A1857" .divider-slide}

## Socio-economic background

- **Disadvantage gap**: ~18 months of learning by GCSE age (EPI annual reports)
  - Narrowed modestly after 2011, partially reversed post-COVID

- **FSM as a proxy**: Widely used but limited
  - Misses nuance of parental social class (Stopforth & Gayle 2025)
  - Threshold changes make comparison harder

- **Concentrations of disadvantage**: Gorard et al. (2019) estimate 0.05--0.15 SD improvement from mixing — ~1 GCSE grade
  - But claim of "almost no cost" overlooks real-world implementation costs

::: {.fragment}
::: {.key-finding}
**Key point:** Inequalities at GCSE are substantially *mediated* by prior attainment at KS2 — most of the gap accumulates *before* secondary school.
:::
:::


## Attendance: the dominant predictor

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

- **DfE 2018/19**: Persistently absent pupils — only 35.6% achieved a standard pass in English & Maths vs 83.7% for no absences

- **DfE 2025**: Near-perfect attendance → **1.9x** as likely to achieve grade 5 pass vs 90--95% attendance

- Social Mobility Commission: correlation "most likely to be **causal**"

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

**Post-pandemic crisis:**

- Persistent absence: 10.5% → **21.9%** in secondary schools
- FSM-eligible pupils: ~**32%** persistently absent
- Absence is socially patterned — compounding disadvantage effects

::: {.key-finding}
Attendance is the strongest individual predictor and perhaps the **ultimate equity lever**.
:::

:::

::::

## Other factors (1)

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

**Prior attainment**

- Reflects accumulated inequalities before secondary school
- Confounds/mediates the effects of deprivation on GCSE outcomes

**Ethnicity**

- Persistent gaps, but substantially reduced when controlling for pupil characteristics


:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
- Disadvantaged White British boys particularly underperforming

**Selectivity**

- Private school premium disappears after controlling for intake
- Grammar schools reinforce socio-economic stratification

:::

::::


## Other factors (2)

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

**Workforce**

- Effective headteachers: ~1 GCSE grade improvement
- Teacher quality: good teachers worth ~4.5 GCSE points
- Teacher turnover reduces attainment, especially for disadvantaged

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

**The London Effect**

- Not really geographic — a collection of *agency factors*: parental expectations, homework hours, lower unauthorised absence
- A cultural difference of attitudes towards education

:::

::::

## The challenge of synthesis

- The evidence base is **vast** — many factors, many studies, many interactions

- Hard for anyone to judge which driver is most important *in a particular context*

- Requires analysis that combines drivers **simultaneously** so relative influences can be assessed

::: {.fragment}
::: {.key-finding}
**This is exactly what was missing in Brighton and Hove.** A single strand of evidence (segregation) was treated as the whole story, to the exclusion of other — potentially more impactful — factors.
:::
:::


# Brighton and Hove: Context {background-color="#3A1857" .divider-slide}

## The city's secondary school landscape

```{r fig-catchments}
#| label: fig-catchments

optionZ <- st_read(
  here::here("data", "optionZ_Mar25.geojson"), quiet = TRUE
) %>% st_transform(4326)

catchment_colours <- c(
  "BACA" = "#e41a1c", "HoveBlatchington" = "#377eb8",
  "Longhill" = "#4daf4a", "PACA" = "#984ea3",
  "Patcham" = "#ff7f00", "VarndeanStringer" = "#a65628"
)
catchment_labels <- c(
  "BACA" = "BACA (Brighton Aldridge)",
  "HoveBlatchington" = "Hove Park / Blatchington Mill",
  "Longhill" = "Longhill", "PACA" = "PACA (Portslade Aldridge)",
  "Patcham" = "Patcham", "VarndeanStringer" = "Varndean / Dorothy Stringer"
)

bh_map_data <- bh_latest %>%
  filter(!is.na(easting), !is.na(northing)) %>%
  st_as_sf(coords = c("easting", "northing"), crs = 27700) %>%
  st_transform(4326)
map_coords <- st_coordinates(bh_map_data)
bh_map_data$lon <- map_coords[, 1]
bh_map_data$lat <- map_coords[, 2]

logo_paths <- c(
  "Blatchington Mill School"                = "output/school_logos/blatchington_mill.png",
  "Brighton Aldridge Community Academy"      = "output/school_logos/brighton_aldridge.png",
  "Cardinal Newman Catholic School"          = "output/school_logos/cardinal_newman.png",
  "Dorothy Stringer School"                  = "output/school_logos/dorothy_stringer.png",
  "Hove Park School and Sixth Form Centre"   = "output/school_logos/hove_park.png",
  "King's School"                            = "output/school_logos/kings_school.png",
  "Longhill High School"                     = "output/school_logos/longhill_high.png",
  "Patcham High School"                      = "output/school_logos/patcham_high.png",
  "Portslade Aldridge Community Academy"     = "output/school_logos/portslade_aldridge.png",
  "Varndean School"                          = "output/school_logos/varndean.png"
)

pal_catch <- colorFactor(
  palette = unname(catchment_colours),
  domain = names(catchment_colours)
)

m_orient <- leaflet(width = "100%", height = 500) %>%
  addProviderTiles(providers$CartoDB.Positron) %>%
  addPolygons(
    data = optionZ,
    fillColor = ~pal_catch(catchment),
    fillOpacity = 0.12,
    color = ~pal_catch(catchment),
    weight = 2.5, opacity = 0.8,
    label = ~catchment_labels[catchment],
    highlightOptions = highlightOptions(weight = 4, fillOpacity = 0.25, bringToFront = FALSE)
  )

for (i in seq_len(nrow(bh_map_data))) {
  sch_name <- bh_map_data$SCHNAME[i]
  sch_lon  <- bh_map_data$lon[i]
  sch_lat  <- bh_map_data$lat[i]

  popup_html <- sprintf(
    "<b>%s</b><br>ATT8: %.1f | FSM: %.1f%% | Absence: %.1f%%",
    sch_name, bh_map_data$ATT8SCR[i],
    bh_map_data$PTFSM6CLA1A[i], bh_map_data$PERCTOT[i]
  )

  m_orient <- m_orient %>%
    addCircleMarkers(
      lng = sch_lon, lat = sch_lat,
      radius = 5, fillColor = "#333333", fillOpacity = 0.9,
      color = "white", weight = 1.5, popup = popup_html
    )

  logo_file <- logo_paths[sch_name]
  if (!is.na(logo_file) && file.exists(logo_file)) {
    logo_b64 <- knitr::image_uri(logo_file)
    logo_icon <- makeIcon(
      iconUrl = logo_b64,
      iconWidth = 36, iconHeight = 36,
      iconAnchorX = -4, iconAnchorY = 40
    )
    m_orient <- m_orient %>%
      addMarkers(lng = sch_lon, lat = sch_lat, icon = logo_icon, popup = popup_html)
  }
}

# CoMArt (Closed 2005)
comart_lon <- -0.099449; comart_lat <- 50.823301
m_orient <- m_orient %>%
  addCircleMarkers(
    lng = comart_lon, lat = comart_lat,
    radius = 5, fillColor = "#e41a1c", fillOpacity = 0.9,
    color = "white", weight = 1.5,
    popup = "<b>CoMArt (Closed 2005)</b>"
  )
comart_logo <- "output/school_logos/comart.png"
if (file.exists(comart_logo)) {
  comart_b64 <- knitr::image_uri(comart_logo)
  comart_icon <- makeIcon(iconUrl = comart_b64, iconWidth = 36, iconHeight = 36,
                           iconAnchorX = -4, iconAnchorY = 40)
  m_orient <- m_orient %>%
    addMarkers(lng = comart_lon, lat = comart_lat, icon = comart_icon,
               popup = "<b>CoMArt (Closed 2005)</b>")
}

# Legend
legend_html <- paste0(
  '<div style="font-size: 12px; line-height: 1.6;">',
  paste0(
    '<div style="display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 6px;">',
    '<span style="display: inline-block; width: 14px; height: 14px; ',
    'background:', unname(catchment_colours), '; ',
    'border-radius: 2px; flex-shrink: 0;"></span>',
    '<span>', unname(catchment_labels), '</span></div>',
    collapse = ''
  ),
  '</div>'
)

m_orient <- m_orient %>%
  addControl(
    html = paste0(
      '<div style="background: white; padding: 8px 12px; ',
      'border-radius: 4px; box-shadow: 0 1px 5px rgba(0,0,0,0.3); ',
      'font-family: sans-serif;">',
      '<div style="font-weight: bold; margin-bottom: 4px; font-size: 12px;">',
      '2025/26 Catchment Boundaries</div>',
      legend_html,
      '</div>'
    ),
    position = "topright"
  )

m_orient
```


## 10 secondary schools — a unique admissions system

- **6 community schools** (LEA-controlled), **2 academies** (BACA, PACA), **2 church schools**

- Catchment areas with **lottery tie-break** for oversubscribed schools (since 2008)
  - Replaced distance-based allocation
  - Very unusual in England

- In 2023, FSM priority added — **already affecting mixing** without opposition

- Brighton & Hove is already in the **top third nationally** for integration of disadvantaged/non-disadvantaged pupils


## The 2024 consultation: the proposals

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

**What was proposed:**

- Reduce PANs at popular central schools (Blatchington Mill, Dorothy Stringer)
- Reserve **20%** of places for out-of-catchment children
- Redraw catchment boundaries in the east

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

**The stated premise:**

> Attainment in the city was *"driven by economic advantage"*

**The logic:** Redistribute disadvantaged pupils more evenly → narrow the attainment gap

**Backup logic:** Give parents more "choice" - implication: "some schools are bad and some want to choose to go elsewhere" - claim affinity with the voiceless

:::

::::


## The 2024 consultation: the response

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

**Public reaction:**

- *"Strong preference for improving existing schools rather than redistributing students"*
- Concerns about community cohesion and student wellbeing
- National media coverage of vocal objections

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

**The institutional context:**

- Labour majority + Leader & Cabinet system
- Compressed consultation timeline
- Limited scope for deliberative engagement
- Privileged access for supportive pressure group, descenting voices marginalised

:::

::::


## The key questions this raises

::: {.incremental}

1. **Is the premise correct?** Is attainment in Brighton & Hove really "driven by economic advantage"?

2. **Is this the right lever?** Even if concentrations of disadvantage matter, are they the *most important* factor?

3. **How is the city actually performing** once structural factors are accounted for?

4. **What are the risks** of pulling this particular lever — including second-order effects?

:::

::: {.fragment}
We can answer all of these with DfE open data.
:::


# Data & Methods {background-color="#3A1857" .divider-slide}

## DfE open data

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

**Data sources:**

- School performance (Attainment 8, Progress 8)
- Pupil absence and attendance
- Pupil characteristics (FSM, EAL, prior attainment)
- School workforce (retention, sickness, pay)
- Ofsted ratings
- Admissions policies
- School characteristics

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

**Our panel:**

- [**13,419 school-year observations**]{.stat-highlight}
- 3,523 academies and maintained schools
- 152 Local Education Authorities
- 4 academic years (2021--22 to 2024--25)
- Post-COVID, post-imputed grades

All publicly available. All linkable. All underutilised.

:::

::::


## Model specification {.smaller}

**Multilevel linear mixed effects model** — schools nested within LEAs within regions:

$$
\log(\text{ATT8}_{ij}) = \beta_0 + \sum_{k=1}^{9} \beta_k \, x_{kij} + u_{\text{year}} + u_{\text{Ofsted}} + u_{\text{region}} + u_{\text{LA|region}} + \varepsilon_{ij}
$$

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

**Fixed effects (predictors):**

- log(% FSM-eligible)
- log(% Overall absence)
- log(% EAL)
- % Low prior attainment (KS2)
- Selective admissions (dummy)
- Gorard Segregation Index (LA-level)
- Teacher retention rate
- Leadership pay proportion
- log(Teacher sickness days)

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

**Random effects (grouping):**

- Academic year
- Ofsted rating (4-category)
- Government Office Region
- LEA nested within region

**Three models:**

- All pupils
- Disadvantaged pupils
- Non-disadvantaged pupils

:::

::::


# National Results {background-color="#3A1857" .divider-slide}

## How good are the models?

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

| Model | Marginal R² | Conditional R² |
|-------|:-----------:|:--------------:|
| All pupils | `r round(r2_all$R2_marginal, 2)` | [**`r round(r2_all$R2_conditional, 2)`**]{.stat-highlight} |
| Disadvantaged | `r round(r2_dis$R2_marginal, 2)` | `r round(r2_dis$R2_conditional, 2)` |
| Non-disadvantaged | `r round(r2_non$R2_marginal, 2)` | `r round(r2_non$R2_conditional, 2)` |

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

::: {.key-finding}
School-level attainment is **remarkably predictable** from a small number of structural factors.

~80% of variation explained for all pupils; ~70--80% for subgroups.
:::

- Disadvantaged pupils: more variation from unmeasured factors (individual resilience, specific interventions)
- Non-disadvantaged: tighter, more predictable

:::

::::


## The mediation story: building the model step by step

```{r tbl-stepwise-slide}
#| label: tbl-stepwise-slide

step_data <- imputed_full_data %>% filter(!is.na(ATT8SCR), ATT8SCR > 0)

m1 <- lm(log(ATT8SCR) ~ log(PTFSM6CLA1A), data = step_data)
m2 <- lm(log(ATT8SCR) ~ log(PTFSM6CLA1A) + log(PERCTOT), data = step_data)
m3 <- lm(log(ATT8SCR) ~ log(PTFSM6CLA1A) + log(PERCTOT) + PTPRIORLO, data = step_data)

get_coef <- function(mod, var) {
  cf <- coef(mod)
  if (var %in% names(cf)) round(cf[var], 3) else NA
}

mediation_tbl <- tibble(
  ` ` = c("FSM coefficient", "Absence coefficient", "Prior att. coefficient", "R²"),
  `M1: FSM only` = c(
    as.character(get_coef(m1, "log(PTFSM6CLA1A)")),
    "—", "—",
    as.character(round(summary(m1)$r.squared, 3))
  ),
  `M2: + Absence` = c(
    as.character(get_coef(m2, "log(PTFSM6CLA1A)")),
    as.character(get_coef(m2, "log(PERCTOT)")),
    "—",
    as.character(round(summary(m2)$r.squared, 3))
  ),
  `M3: + Prior att.` = c(
    as.character(get_coef(m3, "log(PTFSM6CLA1A)")),
    as.character(get_coef(m3, "log(PERCTOT)")),
    as.character(get_coef(m3, "PTPRIORLO")),
    as.character(round(summary(m3)$r.squared, 3))
  )
)

kable(mediation_tbl, align = "lrrr") %>%
  kable_styling(full_width = FALSE) %>%
  row_spec(4, bold = TRUE, background = "#EEDEFF")
```

::: {.key-finding}
Adding absence **halves** the FSM coefficient. Adding prior attainment reduces both further. With just 3 variables → **two-thirds** of variation explained.
:::


## What about segregation?

::: {.incremental}

- The Gorard Segregation Index is **not statistically significant** in the full model

- Once a school's own FSM levels, attendance, prior attainment and geographic location are accounted for → **no additional predictive power**

- Any harm segregation causes is **fully mediated** by the other variables

- This doesn't mean segregation is harmless — it means its effects **operate through** absence, prior attainment, and other measured factors

- We include it because it formed **much of the justification** for the Brighton & Hove proposals

:::


## Relative variable importance

```{r fig-std-coef-slide}
#| label: fig-std-coef-slide
#| fig-height: 5.5

standardised_importance <- function(model, data, outcome_var, group_label) {
  fe <- fixef(model)
  fe <- fe[names(fe) != "(Intercept)"]
  coef_info <- tribble(
    ~term, ~data_col, ~transform,
    "log(PTFSM6CLA1A)", "PTFSM6CLA1A", "log",
    "log(PERCTOT)", "PERCTOT", "log",
    "log(PNUMEAL)", "PNUMEAL", "log",
    "PTPRIORLO", "PTPRIORLO", "none",
    "ADMPOL_PTSEL", "ADMPOL_PT", "dummy_SEL",
    "gorard_segregation", "gorard_segregation", "none",
    "remained_in_the_same_school", "remained_in_the_same_school", "none",
    "teachers_on_leadership_pay_range_percent", "teachers_on_leadership_pay_range_percent", "none",
    "log(average_number_of_days_taken)", "average_number_of_days_taken", "log"
  )
  results <- coef_info %>%
    filter(term %in% names(fe)) %>%
    rowwise() %>%
    mutate(
      coef = fe[term],
      x_vals = list(
        if (transform == "log") log(data[[data_col]][!is.na(data[[data_col]]) & data[[data_col]] > 0])
        else if (transform == "dummy_SEL") as.numeric(data[[data_col]] == "SEL")[!is.na(data[[data_col]])]
        else data[[data_col]][!is.na(data[[data_col]])]
      ),
      sd_x = sd(unlist(x_vals), na.rm = TRUE),
      std_coef = coef * sd_x,
      abs_std_coef = abs(std_coef),
      Group = group_label
    ) %>% ungroup() %>%
    select(term, coef, sd_x, std_coef, abs_std_coef, Group)
  results
}

display_names <- c(
  "log(PTFSM6CLA1A)" = "% Disadvantaged (FSM)",
  "log(PERCTOT)" = "Overall Absence Rate",
  "log(PNUMEAL)" = "% EAL",
  "PTPRIORLO" = "% Low Prior Attainment",
  "ADMPOL_PTSEL" = "Selective Admissions",
  "gorard_segregation" = "Gorard Segregation Index",
  "remained_in_the_same_school" = "Teacher Retention",
  "teachers_on_leadership_pay_range_percent" = "Leadership Pay %",
  "log(average_number_of_days_taken)" = "Teacher Sickness Days"
)

imp_all <- standardised_importance(mod_e_all, imputed_full_data, "ATT8SCR", "All Pupils")
imp_dis <- standardised_importance(mod_e_disadv, imputed_full_data, "ATT8SCR_FSM6CLA1A", "Disadvantaged")
imp_non <- standardised_importance(mod_e_nondisadv, imputed_full_data, "ATT8SCR_NFSM6CLA1A", "Non-Disadvantaged")

importance_national <- bind_rows(imp_all, imp_dis, imp_non) %>%
  mutate(display_name = display_names[term])

var_order <- importance_national %>%
  group_by(display_name) %>%
  summarise(avg_abs = mean(abs_std_coef)) %>%
  arrange(avg_abs) %>% pull(display_name)

importance_national <- importance_national %>%
  mutate(
    display_name = factor(display_name, levels = var_order),
    Group = factor(Group, levels = c("All Pupils", "Disadvantaged", "Non-Disadvantaged"))
  )

ggplot(importance_national, aes(x = display_name, y = std_coef, fill = Group)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), width = 0.7) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40") +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("All Pupils" = "#3A1857",
                                "Disadvantaged" = "#993AFF",
                                "Non-Disadvantaged" = "#30D6FF")) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Standardised Coefficient (signed)", fill = NULL) +
  theme_slide
```


## Key finding: absence dominates

::: {.key-finding}
**Absence is the most powerful predictor** of school-level attainment by a considerable margin — its coefficient is roughly **2.6 times larger** than concentrations of disadvantage.
:::

::: {.incremental}

- For **disadvantaged pupils**, nearly half of school-level performance variation is explained by attendance alone

- Absence affects disadvantaged pupils far more than non-disadvantaged

- Disadvantaged pupils lack safety nets (tutors, engaged parents, revision guides) — they are **more reliant on classroom instruction**

- Attendance is the **ultimate equity lever**

:::


## The contentious finding: concentrations of disadvantage

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="55%"}

For **non-disadvantaged** pupils:

- Higher FSM → lower attainment

For **disadvantaged** pupils:

- Higher FSM → *slightly higher* attainment *(unexpected)*
- After controlling for absence & prior attainment
- Robust across years and alternative specifications

:::

::: {.column width="45%"}

::: {.key-finding}
**Why?** Schools with higher concentrations likely develop specialised support systems — targeted use of Pupil Premium, vocational pathways, specialist staff.

Effect shrinks in the full multilevel model → operates through LEA/Ofsted factors, not concentration *per se*.
:::

:::

::::

::: {.fragment}
We do **not** advocate for concentrating disadvantaged pupils. But deconcentration policies premised on the assumption they inevitably fare worse in higher-poverty schools are **not supported by this evidence**.
:::


## Non-linear effects: context matters

```{r fig-bivariate-slide}
#| label: fig-bivariate-slide
#| fig-height: 4.5

bivar_df <- imputed_full_data %>% filter(!is.na(ATT8SCR), !is.na(PTFSM6CLA1A))

yr_colours <- c("2021-22" = "#3A1857", "2022-23" = "#993AFF",
                "2023-24" = "#30D6FF", "2024-25" = "#B982FF")

# Brighton & Hove schools in latest year
bh_bivar <- bh_latest_model %>%
  filter(!is.na(ATT8SCR), !is.na(PTFSM6CLA1A))

ggplot(bivar_df, aes(x = PTFSM6CLA1A, y = ATT8SCR, colour = year_label)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.25, size = 0.8) +
  geom_smooth(aes(group = 1), method = "loess", se = FALSE,
              colour = "#ED357D", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(data = bh_bivar, aes(x = PTFSM6CLA1A, y = ATT8SCR),
             colour = "black", size = 2, shape = 16, inherit.aes = FALSE) +
  geom_text_repel(data = bh_bivar,
                  aes(x = PTFSM6CLA1A, y = ATT8SCR, label = SCHNAME),
                  colour = "black", size = 2.5, fontface = "bold",
                  max.overlaps = 15, force = 4,
                  segment.colour = "grey30", segment.size = 0.4,
                  inherit.aes = FALSE) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = yr_colours) +
  labs(x = "% Disadvantaged (FSM)", y = "Attainment 8 — All Pupils",
       colour = NULL,
       title = "Above ~20% FSM, the relationship flattens dramatically",
       subtitle = "Brighton & Hove schools (2024-25) shown in black") +
  theme_slide
```

::: {.key-finding}
The same percentage-point change produces very different effects depending on where a school starts. **Where you sit on the curve matters enormously for policy.**
:::


# Brighton & Hove: The Evidence {background-color="#3A1857" .divider-slide}

## How is Brighton & Hove actually performing?

```{r fig-caterpillar-slide}
#| label: fig-caterpillar-slide
#| fig-height: 5.5

re_list_dis <- ranef(mod_e_disadv, condVar = TRUE)
re_la_dis <- as.data.frame(re_list_dis$`LANAME:gor_name`)
re_la_dis$group <- rownames(re_list_dis$`LANAME:gor_name`)
se_la_dis <- sqrt(attr(re_list_dis$`LANAME:gor_name`, "postVar")[1, 1, ])
re_la_dis$se <- se_la_dis

re_la_dis <- re_la_dis %>%
  mutate(
    la_name = sub(":.*$", "", group),
    ci_lo = `(Intercept)` - 1.96 * se,
    ci_hi = `(Intercept)` + 1.96 * se,
    highlight = ifelse(grepl("Brighton", la_name), "Brighton & Hove", "Other")
  )

extreme_la <- c(
  head(re_la_dis$la_name[order(re_la_dis$`(Intercept)`)], 10),
  tail(re_la_dis$la_name[order(re_la_dis$`(Intercept)`)], 10)
)
if (any(grepl("Brighton", re_la_dis$la_name)))
  extreme_la <- unique(c(extreme_la, re_la_dis$la_name[grepl("Brighton", re_la_dis$la_name)]))

ggplot(re_la_dis, aes(x = reorder(group, `(Intercept)`), y = `(Intercept)`)) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = ci_lo, ymax = ci_hi), width = 0, colour = "grey60", linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_point(aes(colour = highlight), size = 1.5) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40") +
  geom_text_repel(
    data = re_la_dis %>% filter(la_name %in% extreme_la),
    aes(label = la_name), size = 3, max.overlaps = 25, force = 4,
    segment.colour = "grey60", segment.size = 0.3
  ) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("Other" = "#B982FF", "Brighton & Hove" = "#ED357D"), guide = "none") +
  labs(title = "LA Random Intercepts — Disadvantaged Pupils",
       subtitle = "Brighton & Hove: 7th best out of 152 LEAs",
       x = NULL, y = "Random Intercept (log scale)") +
  theme_slide +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_blank(), axis.ticks.x = element_blank())
```


## Brighton & Hove: a story of success, not failure

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

**LEA rankings (after structural adjustment):**

| Pupil group | Rank / 152 |
|-------------|:----------:|
| Disadvantaged | [**7th**]{.stat-highlight} |
| Non-disadvantaged | [**5th**]{.stat-highlight} |
| All pupils | [**4th**]{.stat-highlight} |

The positive random intercept ≈ **+2 GCSE points** above what structural factors predict.

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

::: {.key-finding}
**This was entirely unknown** at the time of the 2024 consultation and completely absent from the public narrative.

The starting point was a narrative of *failure*. The evidence says the opposite: this is one of the **highest-performing LEAs in England**.
:::

Rather than "what is the city doing wrong?", the question should be **"what is it doing right?"**

:::

::::


## But there is a problem — and it isn't segregation

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

**FSM (the lever they chose):**

- City mean: `r round(bh_mean_fsm, 1)`%
- National mean: `r round(national_mean_fsm, 1)`%
- National percentile: **`r round(bh_fsm_rank$pctile, 0)`th**
- *Completely unremarkable*

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

**Absence (the lever they ignored):**

- City mean: `r round(bh_mean_abs, 1)`%
- National mean: `r round(national_mean_abs, 1)`%
- National percentile: [**`r round(bh_abs_rank$pctile, 0)`th**]{.stat-highlight}
- *Among the very worst in England*

:::

::::

::: {.fragment}
::: {.key-finding}
Brighton & Hove has **near-average disadvantage** but **near-worst absence**. The city's strong underlying performance is being dragged down by an absence problem at the extreme of the national distribution.
:::
:::


## Absence league table

```{r tbl-absence-slide}
#| label: tbl-absence-slide

all_years <- sort(unique(national_benchmarks$year_label))

la_abs_all_years <- national_benchmarks %>%
  group_by(year_label, LANAME) %>%
  summarise(la_mean_abs = mean(PERCTOT, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  group_by(year_label) %>%
  arrange(la_mean_abs) %>%
  mutate(rank = row_number(), n_las = n()) %>%
  ungroup()

top10_any_year <- la_abs_all_years %>%
  group_by(year_label) %>%
  slice_max(la_mean_abs, n = 10) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  pull(LANAME) %>% unique()
top10_any_year <- unique(c(top10_any_year,
  la_abs_all_years$LANAME[grepl("Brighton", la_abs_all_years$LANAME)]))

abs_wide <- la_abs_all_years %>%
  filter(LANAME %in% top10_any_year) %>%
  mutate(display = paste0(format(round(la_mean_abs, 1), nsmall = 1),
                           "% (", rank, "/", n_las, ")")) %>%
  select(LANAME, year_label, display) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = year_label, values_from = display, values_fill = "\u2014")

latest_order <- la_abs_all_years %>%
  filter(year_label == latest_year, LANAME %in% top10_any_year) %>%
  arrange(desc(la_mean_abs))

abs_wide <- abs_wide %>%
  mutate(LANAME = factor(LANAME, levels = latest_order$LANAME)) %>%
  arrange(LANAME) %>%
  mutate(LANAME = as.character(LANAME))

bh_row <- which(grepl("Brighton", abs_wide$LANAME))
names(abs_wide)[1] <- "Local Authority"

kable(abs_wide, align = paste0("l", paste(rep("r", length(all_years)), collapse = ""))) %>%
  kable_styling(full_width = FALSE) %>%
  row_spec(bh_row, bold = TRUE, background = "#EEDEFF")
```


## Accelerating returns: comparing the two levers

```{r fig-accelerating-slide}
#| label: fig-accelerating-slide
#| fig-height: 5

beta_fsm     <- fixef(mod_e_all)["log(PTFSM6CLA1A)"]
beta_absence <- fixef(mod_e_all)["log(PERCTOT)"]
city_att8    <- mean(bh_latest_model$ATT8SCR, na.rm = TRUE)

fsm_range <- seq(5, 55, by = 0.5)
abs_range <- seq(3, 16, by = 0.1)

marginal_fsm <- tibble(
  starting_value = fsm_range,
  att8_gain = city_att8 * (exp(beta_fsm * (log(fsm_range - 1) - log(fsm_range))) - 1),
  Variable = "% FSM (Disadvantaged)")

marginal_abs <- tibble(
  starting_value = abs_range,
  att8_gain = city_att8 * (exp(beta_absence * (log(abs_range - 1) - log(abs_range))) - 1),
  Variable = "% Absence")

marginal_df <- bind_rows(marginal_fsm, marginal_abs)

bh_fsm_pts <- bh_latest_model %>%
  filter(!is.na(PTFSM6CLA1A), PTFSM6CLA1A > 2) %>%
  transmute(School = SCHNAME, starting_value = PTFSM6CLA1A,
            att8_gain = city_att8 * (exp(beta_fsm * (log(PTFSM6CLA1A - 1) - log(PTFSM6CLA1A))) - 1),
            Variable = "% FSM (Disadvantaged)")

bh_abs_pts <- bh_latest_model %>%
  filter(!is.na(PERCTOT), PERCTOT > 2) %>%
  transmute(School = SCHNAME, starting_value = PERCTOT,
            att8_gain = city_att8 * (exp(beta_absence * (log(PERCTOT - 1) - log(PERCTOT))) - 1),
            Variable = "% Absence")

bh_pts <- bind_rows(bh_fsm_pts, bh_abs_pts)

ggplot(marginal_df, aes(x = starting_value, y = att8_gain, colour = Variable, linetype = Variable)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(data = bh_pts, aes(x = starting_value, y = att8_gain), size = 2.5, alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_text_repel(data = bh_pts,
    aes(x = starting_value, y = att8_gain, label = School),
    size = 2.8, max.overlaps = 15, force = 3,
    segment.colour = "grey60", segment.size = 0.3, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = national_mean_fsm, linetype = "dotted", colour = "#3A1857", linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = national_mean_abs, linetype = "dotted", colour = "#005E5C", linewidth = 0.4) +
  annotate("segment", x = 45, xend = 15, y = max(marginal_df$att8_gain) * 0.92,
           yend = max(marginal_df$att8_gain) * 0.92,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.2, "cm")), colour = "grey50", linewidth = 0.4) +
  annotate("text", x = 30, y = max(marginal_df$att8_gain) * 0.85,
           label = "direction of improvement", colour = "grey50", size = 3.5, fontface = "italic") +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("% FSM (Disadvantaged)" = "#3A1857", "% Absence" = "#005E5C")) +
  scale_linetype_manual(values = c("% FSM (Disadvantaged)" = "dashed", "% Absence" = "solid")) +
  guides(colour = guide_legend(""), linetype = guide_legend("")) +
  labs(x = "Current level (%)", y = "Predicted ATT8 gain from 1pp reduction",
       title = "Absence delivers larger gains at every starting level") +
  theme_slide
```


## What would closing the absence gap deliver?

```{r tbl-absence-scenario-slide}
#| label: tbl-absence-scenario-slide

beta_abs_dis <- fixef(mod_e_disadv)["log(PERCTOT)"]

above_nat_abs <- bh_latest_model %>%
  filter(!is.na(PERCTOT), PERCTOT > national_mean_abs, !is.na(ATT8SCR)) %>%
  arrange(desc(PERCTOT)) %>%
  transmute(
    School = SCHNAME,
    `Current Absence %` = round(PERCTOT, 1),
    `National Avg (Target)` = round(national_mean_abs, 1),
    `Reduction (pp)` = round(PERCTOT - national_mean_abs, 1),
    `ATT8 gain (all)` = round(ATT8SCR * (exp(beta_absence * (log(national_mean_abs) - log(PERCTOT))) - 1), 1),
    `ATT8 gain (disadv.)` = round(
      ifelse(!is.na(ATT8SCR_FSM6CLA1A) & ATT8SCR_FSM6CLA1A > 0,
             ATT8SCR_FSM6CLA1A * (exp(beta_abs_dis * (log(national_mean_abs) - log(PERCTOT))) - 1), NA), 1)
  )

kable(above_nat_abs) %>%
  kable_styling(full_width = FALSE) %>%
  column_spec(5, bold = TRUE) %>%
  column_spec(6, bold = TRUE, color = "#3A1857")
```

::: {.key-finding}
Bringing absence to the national average → predicted gains of **3--5 GCSE points** at the worst-affected schools. FSM reductions → closer to **1--2 points**.
:::


## Observed vs predicted: Brighton & Hove schools (2024--25)

```{r fig-obs-pred-slide}
#| label: fig-obs-pred-slide
#| fig-height: 6

latest_yr <- max(bh_data$year_label, na.rm = TRUE)

national_2425 <- imputed_full_data %>%
  filter(year_label == latest_yr) %>%
  mutate(
    pred_all = predict(mod_e_all, newdata = ., allow.new.levels = TRUE),
    pred_dis = predict(mod_e_disadv, newdata = ., allow.new.levels = TRUE),
    pred_non = predict(mod_e_nondisadv, newdata = ., allow.new.levels = TRUE),
    is_bh = grepl("Brighton", LANAME)
  )

outcomes <- list(
  list(obs = "ATT8SCR", pred = "pred_all", label = "All Pupils"),
  list(obs = "ATT8SCR_FSM6CLA1A", pred = "pred_dis", label = "Disadvantaged"),
  list(obs = "ATT8SCR_NFSM6CLA1A", pred = "pred_non", label = "Non-Disadvantaged")
)

nat_long <- map_dfr(outcomes, function(o) {
  national_2425 %>%
    filter(!is.na(.data[[o$obs]]), !is.na(.data[[o$pred]])) %>%
    transmute(School = SCHNAME, LANAME, Group = o$label,
              Observed = .data[[o$obs]], Predicted = exp(.data[[o$pred]]),
              is_bh = is_bh)
}) %>%
  mutate(Group = factor(Group, levels = c("All Pupils", "Disadvantaged", "Non-Disadvantaged")))

rng <- range(c(nat_long$Observed, nat_long$Predicted), na.rm = TRUE)
rng <- c(max(0, rng[1] - 2), rng[2] + 2)
bh_points <- nat_long %>% filter(is_bh)

ggplot(nat_long, aes(x = Predicted, y = Observed)) +
  geom_point(data = nat_long %>% filter(!is_bh), colour = "grey80", size = 0.5, alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, colour = "grey40", linewidth = 0.4, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_point(data = bh_points, colour = "#ED357D", size = 2.5, alpha = 0.9) +
  geom_text_repel(data = bh_points, aes(label = School), colour = "#993AFF",
    size = 2.8, max.overlaps = 20, force = 6, segment.colour = "grey50", segment.size = 0.3) +
  facet_wrap(~ Group, ncol = 3) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = rng, ylim = rng) +
  labs(x = "Predicted ATT8", y = "Observed ATT8",
       title = "Observed vs Predicted — Brighton & Hove highlighted") +
  theme_slide +
  theme(panel.spacing = unit(1, "lines"), strip.text = element_text(size = 12))
```


## Alternative league tables: value-added

```{r league-tables-slide}
#| label: league-tables-slide

outcomes_bh <- list(
  list(obs = "ATT8SCR", pred = "predicted_all", label = "All Pupils"),
  list(obs = "ATT8SCR_FSM6CLA1A", pred = "predicted_dis", label = "Disadvantaged"),
  list(obs = "ATT8SCR_NFSM6CLA1A", pred = "predicted_non", label = "Non-Disadvantaged")
)

bh_long <- map_dfr(outcomes_bh, function(o) {
  bh_data %>%
    filter(!is.na(!!sym(o$obs)), !is.na(!!sym(o$pred))) %>%
    transmute(School = SCHNAME, Year = year_label, Group = o$label,
              Observed = !!sym(o$obs), Predicted = exp(!!sym(o$pred)),
              Residual = Observed - exp(!!sym(o$pred)))
}) %>%
  mutate(Group = factor(Group, levels = c("All Pupils", "Disadvantaged", "Non-Disadvantaged")))

format_resid <- function(x) {
  ifelse(is.na(x), "\u2014",
    ifelse(x > 0,
      sprintf('<span style="color:#00A19D;font-weight:bold">%+.1f</span>', x),
      sprintf('<span style="color:#781C1C;font-weight:bold">%+.1f</span>', x)))
}

# Disadvantaged table
panel_dis <- bh_long %>% filter(Group == "Disadvantaged")
panel_dis_wide <- panel_dis %>%
  select(School, Year, Residual) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = Year, values_from = Residual) %>%
  left_join(
    panel_dis %>% group_by(School) %>%
      summarise(`Mean` = mean(Residual, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop"),
    by = "School") %>%
  arrange(desc(Mean)) %>%
  mutate(Rank = row_number()) %>% relocate(Rank)

panel_dis_display <- panel_dis_wide %>%
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric) & !matches("Rank"), ~ format_resid(.x)))

kable(panel_dis_display, format = "html", escape = FALSE,
      caption = "Disadvantaged Pupils: Value-Added Rankings (ATT8 points vs prediction)") %>%
  kable_styling(full_width = FALSE) %>%
  column_spec(ncol(panel_dis_display), bold = TRUE, background = "#EEDEFF")
```


## The BACA paradox

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

**What people thought:**

- 'Requires Improvement' Ofsted
- Low raw attainment scores
- **→ narrative of "Avoid this school"**
- Equity in Education campaign: parents opt for Patcham instead

**What the data shows:**

- [**Best performing school in the city**]{.stat-highlight} for disadvantaged pupils
- +5 GCSE points above national peers (2024--25)
- April 2025: Ofsted upgraded to **'Good'** in all areas

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

**Patcham — "the safer choice":**

- Rated 'Good' by Ofsted
- Comfortable mid-table in raw terms
- But **negative value-added** for disadvantaged pupils
- 4-year average: **--2.6 GCSE points**

::: {.key-finding}
Parents, acting rationally on available information, may have been choosing the school with *lower* value-added for their children — if that is the most important factor. They were operating in a **knowledge vacuum**.
:::

:::

::::


## Attendance as a school-level lever

- **Patcham** is one of only **two** schools in the city with attendance better than the national average

- This is an *actionable lever* at the school level

- BACA exemplifies the national finding: schools with higher disadvantage can develop specialised, effective support

- Every school in the city could learn from Patcham on attendance — and from BACA on supporting disadvantaged pupils

::: {.key-finding}
The city needs to **disaggregate the attendance problem** — different causes require different strategies: family holidays, SEND challenges, transport barriers, caring responsibilities.
:::


# Discussion & Reflections {background-color="#3A1857" .divider-slide}

## The danger of single-lever policy thinking

::: {.incremental}

- The council's proposals were built on the premise that attainment was **"driven by economic advantage"**

- This premise is false in the context of the DfE's own research (Macleod et al. 2015) — our modelling confirms this was **at best a flawed understanding**

- Concentrations of disadvantage are one of the **weaker** levers available

- For disadvantaged pupils specifically, the direction of effect **runs contrary** to the policy assumptions - across England over 4-years of data, disadvantaged pupils get better results in schools with higher concentrations of disadvantaged pupils

- The policy was pulling a lever with relatively little mechanical advantage while ignoring one with **considerably more**

:::


## Second-order effects: the risks of the chosen lever

- The chosen policy if implemented as intended requires **longer journeys** for many children

- @Thomson2023: pupils who travel further to school are **absent more often**

- Given absence is the city's **most acute problem** and the **strongest predictor** of attainment...

::: {.key-finding}
A policy that even marginally increases absence could be **counterproductive** in attainment terms. The claim of "almost no cost" for social mixing overlooks these second-order effects.
:::

::: {.fragment}

Following opposition and appeals: PAN reductions rejected, 20% out-of-catchment → 5%. But the policy reverberations continue through **parental choices** already made. **Little displacement on 2026 offer day luck not judgement!**

:::


## What we don't know

The LEA random effect is **large and significant** — something about Brighton & Hove's schools is delivering results well above prediction. But what?

::: {.incremental}

- High levels of **parental engagement and aspiration**? (well-educated population)
- Quality of **school leadership and governance**?
- Effective **local authority support** services?
- **Collaborative relationships** between schools?
- **Community factors** difficult to quantify?

:::

::: {.fragment}
::: {.key-finding}
Identifying sources of over-performance is a key research question. But **policy interventions that risk disrupting a well-functioning system without understanding what makes it function well carry substantial downside risk**.
:::
:::


## What should Brighton and Hove do? {.smaller}

The council has recently agreed to a **cross-party working group** on schools — a positive step. Our strongest recommendation:

::: {.key-finding}
**Priority one: a deep dive into absence.** The city has the 2nd worst absence rate in England but near-average disadvantage. This is not inevitable — it is, in principle, solvable.
:::

The absence problem needs **disaggregating** — very different situations exist within the headline statistics:

- A few days missed for a family holiday by an otherwise well-attending pupil
- A SEND student who struggles with the school environment but has strong family support
- A student with caring responsibilities living far from school, struggling with bus journeys
- Post-pandemic disengagement and mental health challenges

Each requires a **different strategy**. Schools like Patcham — one of only two with attendance below the national average — offer lessons in what works locally.


## Lessons for other LEAs

::: {.incremental}

- Every LEA has a **different profile** of challenges — different positions along non-linear curves

- What works in one context may be **irrelevant or counterproductive** in another

- The DfE open data provides the **raw materials** for local understanding

- But there's a crucial gap between **raw data** and **actionable intelligence**

- **National ambitions will founder** if pursued locally without adequate analytical infrastructure

:::


# The Policy Simulator Tool {background-color="#3A1857" .divider-slide}

## Bridging the gap

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

**What it does:**

- Search for **any school** in England
- See key statistics and contextual benchmarks
- Compare **observed vs predicted** attainment
- **Simulate** the impact of changes to absence, FSM, workforce etc.
- Explore **LA-level** patterns and typologies
- Find a school's contextual **"twin"**

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

**Built with:**

- R Shiny
- Same multilevel models as this analysis
- DfE open data (updated annually)
- Development accelerated by **Claude** (Anthropic) — AI4CI funded

:::

::::

::: {.key-finding}
Not the finished article — but a demonstration of what's possible when open data + appropriate methods + accessible tools come together.
:::


## The simulator in action

![](output/figures/policy_sim.png){fig-align="center" height="400"}

:::{.smaller}
School Finder → select any school → view contextual statistics → send to the Policy Simulator
:::

[adam-dennett.shinyapps.io/School_Attainment_Policy_Simulator](https://adam-dennett.shinyapps.io/School_Attainment_Policy_Simulator/)

## Adjusting policy levers

![](output/figures/policy_sim_sliders.png){fig-align="center" height="400"}

:::{.smaller}
Non-linear effects mean the same adjustment produces **different impacts** depending on the school's starting position
:::


# Conclusions & Recommendations {background-color="#3A1857" .divider-slide}

## Key takeaways

::: {.incremental}

1. School-level attainment is **remarkably predictable** (~80% variance explained) from a handful of open data variables

2. **Absence is the dominant predictor** — 2.6x more important than concentrations of disadvantage

3. For disadvantaged pupils, the concentration effect **runs in the opposite direction** to common assumption

4. The Gorard Segregation Index is **not significant** once other factors are controlled for

5. Brighton & Hove is one of the **highest-performing LEAs** — but has the **2nd worst absence** in England

6. The 2024 policy was **pulling the wrong lever** — targeting the weaker factor while ignoring the stronger one

7. Crude metrics (raw league tables, single-word Ofsted) **mislead** parents and policy makers

:::


## Recommendations

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="33%"}

**For the DfE:**

- Invest in **analytical infrastructure** at the local level
- Fund **LA analytical capacity** or develop nationally maintained benchmarking tools
- Go **beyond raw league tables**

:::

::: {.column width="33%"}

**For LEAs:**

- **Resist single causal narratives**
- The factors are multiple, interacting, non-linear, context-dependent
- Create institutional space for **genuine deliberation** before irreversible decisions
- Prioritise **attendance** as a policy lever

:::

::: {.column width="33%"}

**For schools, governors & parents:**

- **Contextualised benchmarking** offers a fairer basis than raw scores
- Demand **better information** before making choices
- School 'quality' ≠ raw attainment

:::

::::


## The final word

::: {.key-finding}

**The caution:** Policy made in haste, anchored to a single causal narrative and implemented without adequate regard for evidence, risks doing more harm than good — particularly when it disrupts a system that is working well.

:::

::: {.fragment}

::: {.key-finding}

**The encouragement:** The raw materials for better policy **already exist** in DfE open data. With the right analytical tools and a commitment to evidence over ideology, it is possible to understand with considerable precision what drives attainment in any local context — and where the most productive interventions lie.

:::

:::

::: {.fragment}

::: {.key-finding}

**The challenge:** Ensuring this understanding reaches decision makers in a usable form **before** decisions are made — not after.

:::

:::


## Links & resources {background-color="#EEDEFF"}

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

**The tool:**

🔗 [Policy Simulator](https://adam-dennett.shinyapps.io/School_Attainment_Policy_Simulator/)

**The analysis:**

🔗 [Full analysis & supplementary material](https://adamdennett.github.io/school_attainment_tool/)

**The code:**

🔗 [GitHub repository](https://github.com/adamdennett/school_attainment_tool)

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

**The paper:**

🔗 [Full report](https://adamdennett.github.io/school_attainment_tool/pulling_the_right_lever.html)

**Contact:**

📧 a.dennett@ucl.ac.uk

**Funding:**

🔗 [UKRI AI4CI Hub](https://ai4ci.ac.uk/) — National AI Research Hub for Collective Intelligence

:::

::::

:::{.smallest}
All data used in this analysis are publicly available from the Department for Education. All processing code is open source.
:::
